seoTitle: Fractional SaaS MVP: feasibility, phases, backlog | Luc Rousseau
seoDescription: Fractional SaaS or MVP mandate: four steps, CEO/CTO validation (Lovable, Cursor), phases, backlog, and steering devs or agency. Go/no-go and stack before big spend.

hero.quote: I have a clear idea, a budget, and zero interest in spending 6 months building something nobody wants.
hero.title: Launching a SaaS or MVP
hero.lede:
You're preparing an MVP or SaaS and want to frame before investing heavily. Idea, market, sometimes budget or agency in sight, but no clear map yet: stack, first-release scope, risks, what to validate before months of dev.

I work in phased mandates with the CEO, CTO, or founders: feasibility, go/no-go, architecture, rapid prototypes (Lovable, Claude, Cursor: you prototype, I guide). I can also steer your devs or agency: define phases, structure the backlog, prioritize what you need for an MVP that works without derailing. Not an embedded CTO: direction, sequence, and product-tech coherence, not day-to-day ticket management.

Fractional engagement, not full-time: typically half-days to frame and stay aligned, full days when needed for intensive prototype or architecture work, sometimes two days a week if I code key building blocks. Rhythm is set on the call, phase by phase.

blocks.title: Build too early, validate too late
blocks.lede:
Without framing, v1 drifts: scope swells, default stack, agency or devs coding while what and why still move. Fixing after three months of build costs far more than a few weeks of initial framing.

AI and tools like Lovable, Claude, or Cursor let you prototype fast: the stake is no longer waiting months to see if the idea holds, but validating before you commit heavily on time, team, and money.

blocks.title: Validate with the CEO or CTO before the big build
blocks.intro: Close work with leadership and tech: you can prototype yourself with the right tools; I frame the sequence so experiments support decisions, not a future debt pile.

panels.heading: Fast prototypes, guided
items: CEO, CTO, or founders: we define which hypotheses to test before build
Lovable, Claude, Cursor, or similar: you explore, I guide scope, criteria, and next step
Clickable flows and POCs in days to test and validate, not months of fuzzy specs

panels.heading: Less risk before time, team, and budget
items: A framed, validated idea: investing in dev resources is much less risky
I steer the sequence so experiments don't scatter in every direction
Written phases, prioritized backlog, and steering devs or agency when build starts

blocks.title: Fractional, phased, with a plan
blocks.lede: Not an embedded CTO or an agency that starts build without a map: phased, part-time mandate. Framing often on half-days; a full day for intensive prototype or architecture; two days a week possible on a focused build phase. Calendar set on the call.

items.title: I code, steer devs or agency
items.content: Solo founder: prototypes and key blocks on demand. With devs or agency: I define phases, own the backlog, and prioritize the MVP, without micro-managing day-to-day tickets.

items.title: Feasibility before the big build
items.content: Documented go/no-go, realistic stack, sharp v1 scope. You avoid funding months of build on fuzzy or never-validated assumptions with real users.

items.title: Phases, backlog, and MVP priorities
items.content: Written delivery phases, structured backlog, must-have vs later: a plan for a working MVP your devs or agency can execute without scope spinning out of control.

items.title: Fast prototypes, CEO or CTO
items.content: Lovable, Claude, Cursor: you prototype yourself; I structure hypotheses, scope, and criteria. Validation before big spend, without unmanageable technical debt.

items.title: Sequence and clear guardrails
items.content: Hypotheses, success criteria, throwaway vs prod: I hold the frame so fast experiments stay scoped and don't leave unmanageable debt behind you.

blocks.title: Four typical steps in an MVP or SaaS mandate

items.title: Feasibility and product direction
items.content: Hypotheses, constraints, technical options, ballpark, go/no-go. Deliverable: documented decision, not a vague deck.

items.title: Architecture and v1 scope
items.content: Boundaries, APIs, stack (Laravel, React/Next, headless if relevant). Realistic MVP: sharp v1, first phases and backlog sketched before the big build.

items.title: Prototypes and prerequisites
items.content: With the CEO or CTO: clickable flows, POCs via Lovable, Claude, Cursor, or targeted build. You prototype, I guide; acceptance criteria and validation before big budget. Agency or team gets a brief ready to build, not fuzzy specs.

items.title: Handover and optional follow-up
items.content: Documentation, prioritized backlog, pass-off or ongoing steering of your devs or agency in slices. Part-time follow-up to keep a coherent MVP during build, without a permanent role.

blocks.title: Build straight with agency/devs vs product-system framing first
blocks.intro: This comparison is about launching a SaaS or MVP (feasibility, stack, prototypes). Different from Post-funding: before your first full-time developer, for startups preparing a first tech hire rather than framing a product to launch. AI-centric offer: see AI in your product.

headers.buildFirst: Agency brief or first devs immediately

rows.phaseFirst: v1 scope and hypotheses tested before big commit.

rows.buildFirst: Often agency default or dev preference.
rows.phaseFirst: Choice tied to product and operations, documented.
Early CEO/CTO prototypes (Lovable, Cursor, etc.), validation before big budget.
Written phases, prioritized backlog, steering devs or agency.
Same person from direction through build, not team manager.

blocks.title: Technical foundations
blocks.lede: MVP and SaaS framed before big spend: realistic stack, sharp scope, and clean handover to your devs or agency.
items: Guided rapid prototyping (Lovable, Claude, Cursor) with CEO or CTO
Steering in-house devs or agency (product mode)
Next.js, React, or Vue for the front
Laravel and APIs for the backend

items.title: We already have an agency that can do everything
items.content: Better still: I define phases, structure the backlog, prioritize MVP must-haves, and steer the agency on direction, prerequisites, and coherence. They execute; I don't replace the team.

items.title: How long before we « really » code?
items.content: Often a few weeks of framing for more predictable months of build. On high-leverage topics I can ship a POC; I don't take every ticket for you.

items.title: What rhythm: half-days, full days, two days a week?
items.content: It depends on the phase. Half-days for initial framing, CEO/CTO workshops, and light follow-up. A full day now and then for a prototype or architecture sprint. Two days a week only if I need to code foundations or critical POCs. No 40-hour role: we calibrate on the call.

items.title: Can we prototype ourselves with Lovable or Cursor?
items.content: Yes, and it's often a lever. I work with the CEO, CTO, or founders: you explore with Lovable, Claude, Cursor, or similar; I guide so it serves product and technical validation, not spaghetti to ship as-is to prod. Once the idea is framed and validated, investing in team and budget is much less risky.

items.title: Do you code yourself or steer our team?
items.content: Both. I code prototypes and key blocks when needed. I steer your devs or agency: phases, backlog, prioritization for an MVP that holds, product-tech reviews and trade-offs, without taking every ticket for you.

items.title: Will you become our CTO?
items.content: No. Your agency or devs keep operational pace. I clarify system, v1 scope, and sequence; I don't manage people or day-to-day tickets.

items.title: We can't afford to slow down for framing
items.content: Two to four weeks of framing often avoid months of rework. The first slice can be exactly that framing, with a written deliverable before the big build spend.

items.title: What if we raise after?
items.content: You arrive with plan, stack, and validation proof, less embarrassing technical due diligence. I can help sharpen the first tech or product hire profile (see Post-funding: before your first full-time developer).

items.title: What happens after framing?
items.content: Documented handover to your devs or agency; optional part-time follow-up if you want coherence without a permanent role. Other slice-based mandates: WordPress rebuild, editorial platform, or all situations.

items.title: Reachable outside scheduled days, or in an emergency?
items.content:
The agreed rhythm structures deep work, not whether you can reach me.

Ongoing, I make time to reply on Slack or your preferred channel. Out-of-scope emergencies go through the shared urgency frame we define together (scope, priority, response time).

blocks.label: Discuss MVP or SaaS
blocks.teaser: SaaS idea without a clear map? One call to see if a slice-based mandate (framing → architecture → prerequisites) avoids premature build.
